criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The initial assessment results in understanding of all previous care
and the care that the family is presently seeking.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The initial assessment results in a diagnosis and the formulation of an individualised plan of care for the child and family.
criteria.guideline: If the initial assessment reveals any concern that requires referral to the legal system (for example, neglect or abuse) these must be addressed in the plan of care and implemented according to legal requirements and organisation policy. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation identifies patients in pain during the initial assessment process.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation identifies those patient populations and special situations for which the initial assessment process is modified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence in the form of a detailed policy and procedure or guideline is provided. To ensure coordination of patient care, all members of the team should be included in the formulation of this policy framework, especially doctors and nurses. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Those special patient populations receive individualised assessments.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The findings of assessments performed outside the organisation are verified on admission.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Any significant changes in the patient’s condition since the assessment performed outside of the organisation are noted in the patient’s record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: A process is in place to identify needs for discharge planning at the initial assessment.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: Healthcare professionals responsible for patient care collaborate to   analyse and integrate assessment information
standards.intent: Patients benefit most when the personnel responsible for their care work together to analyse the assessment findings and to combine this information into a comprehensive picture of his or her condition. From this collaboration, the patient's needs are identified, the order of their importance is established and care decisions are made.

criteria.criterion_text: Assessment findings are documented in the patient's record and are readily available to those responsible for the patient’s care.
criteria.guideline: There must be evidence that all clinical professionals dealing with the patient have contributed to the patient's record. If the doctors' (or other health care practitioners') clinical notes are not available in the patient records, this criterion is scored PC. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient assessment data and information are analysed and integrated by those responsible for the patient's care.
criteria.guideline: Those involved in the patient's care form the multidisciplinary team. The constitution of the team will vary for each patient and may include representatives from the medical, nursing, social work, psychology, occupational therapy, physiotherapy and other disciplines, departments or services. Information from all disciplines involved in the patient's care should be considered when developing the treatment plan. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient needs are prioritized based on assessment results.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The patient and/or his or her family participate in the decisions regarding the priority needs to be met.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and/or the patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient Care

categories.category_title: Patient Care

standards.standard_title: Adequate resources are available for the provision of safe care to patients in the service.
standards.intent:
In order to provide safe patient care, each unit requires adequate resources. The physical facilities required include adequate office accommodation for personnel, sluice rooms which are hygienically clean, treatment and dressing rooms, and adequate storage space for clean linen.
Counseling and treatment rooms, group rooms, classrooms, a dining room, an out-door play area, structured activity programmes and age-appropriate toys and games should also be available.
Cleaning equipment is safely stored in a room or cupboard expressly for this purpose. No cleaning equipment should be stored in areas where clean linen, medical supplies or food are stored. There are adequate toilet and bathing facilities for the number of patients in the ward as determined by country specific legislation.
There is adequate lighting and ventilation. Nurse call systems are available at bedsides and in bathrooms and toilets and is connected to the emergency power supply.
There is at least one oxygen and one vacuum point for every 2 beds. Where there is no piped oxygen and vacuum supply, there are mobile oxygen and vacuum pumps. All necessary fittings for oxygen and suction are in place and working. Each bed is serviced by at least one electrical socket outlet. Each ward is provided with a socket outlet which is connected to the emergency power supply.
Every patient care area must have access to resuscitation equipment within one minute of any patient collapsing that includes as a minimum:
Defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) with adult and paediatric paddles/pads available within three minutes of any patient collapsing
ECG monitor
CPR board
Suction apparatus, hard and soft suction catheters and suction tubing
Paediatric and adult bag-mask manual ventilator (Ambubag) or equivalent
A range of endotracheal tubes and two laryngoscopes, with a range of straight and curved blades, spare batteries and spare globes where applicable
An introducer/stylet for endotracheal intubation
A syringe to inflate the endotracheal tube cuff
A paediatric laryngeal mask airway
Oral airways
Equipment to perform an emergency cricothyroidotomy
Medication for cardiac and respiratory arrest, coma, fits and states of shock (including paediatric doses)
Appropriate consumables for intravenous therapy and medication administration and volume expanders (including paediatric sizes)

criteria.criterion_text: Patient and personnel accommodation in the service is adequate to meet patient care needs.

ROOTThere should be adequate office space for personnel; clean, hygienic sluice rooms, treatment and dressing rooms; adequate, tidy linen and equipment storage facilities; suitable lighting and ventilation; adequate, hygienic bathroom and toilet facilities for the number of patients, as well as the requirements mentioned below.ROOTThere should be adequate office space for personnel; clean, hygienic sluice rooms, treatment and dressing rooms; adequate, tidy linen and equipment storage facilities; suitable lighting and ventilation; adequate, hygienic bathroom and toilet facilities for the number of patients, as well as the requirements mentioned below.
There is evidence that equipment is maintained in accordance with the policies of the organisation.
criteria.guideline: This refers to medical equipment and not to furniture such as beds.

criteria.criterion_text: Resuscitation equipment is available in accordance with the policies of the organisation and includes a) – m) in the standard intent above.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Resuscitation equipment and documented evidence of the checking thereof in the unit, according to the organisation's resuscitation policy must be available. Checking must include the identification of expiry dates on consumables such as airways and endotracheal tubes. Documented evidence of this checking is required. A resuscitation trolley must be available at the point of need within one minute. In addition, there must be access to a defibrillator or automated external defibrillator (AED) within three minutes of any patient collapsing. The 40/80 rule for compliance will apply.

criteria.criterion_text: Each patient has access to a nurse call system at all times.
criteria.guideline: A functional nurse call system must be available at each patient's bed and in the ablution facilities. Random tests can be conducted to assess function and response. Assessment will be made on the response from patient interviews and patient complaints. This criterion will be scored accordingly, for example, NC if there is no functional call system and PC if nurses do not respond.

criteria.criterion_text: Oxygen and vacuum supplies meet patient care requirements.
criteria.guideline: There should be either piped oxygen/vacuum or mobile oxygen cylinders and vacuum pumps in working order to meet the patients' needs. This includes the availability of a sufficient number of functional flow-meters. Any evidence of infection control deficiencies for example, stagnant water in humidifiers/vacuum bottles will be linked to Prevention and Control of Infections.

criteria.criterion_text: When patients receive oxygen from a cylinder, the cylinder pressures are monitored according to organisation policy.
criteria.guideline: This applies to situations when patients are given oxygen by cylinder, for example, where no piped oxygen is available or in the event that the piped oxygen system fails. The cylinder pressure indicates the remaining contents of the cylinder. Monitoring the contents will alert personnel to the need for the cylinder to be replaced and prevent the interruption of the patient's oxygen supply.

criteria.criterion_text: Electricity and water is available in accordance with the policies and arrangements of the organisation.
criteria.guideline: Ward/unit personnel should have knowledge of routine arrangements and the contingency plans.

standards.standard_title: The care provided to each patient is planned and written in the patient's record
standards.intent:
A single, integrated plan is preferable to the entry of a separate care plan recorded by each healthcare professional. The care plan should include the care to be delivered and the intended goals of care.
Collaborative care and treatment team meetings or similar patient discussions are recorded. Diagnostic and other procedures must be ordered by individuals qualified to do so. These orders must be easily accessible if they are to be acted on in a timely manner. Locating orders on a common sheet or in a uniform location in patient records facilitates the correct understanding and carrying out of orders. The reason for requesting diagnostic imaging or laboratory tests should be recorded.
An organisation decides:
Which orders must be written rather than verbal
Who is permitted to write orders
Where orders are to be located in the patient record
The method used must respect the confidentiality of patient care information, for example, order books must not be kept in an area where they can be seen by other patients or visitors and verbal orders, including those given over the telephone, should be taken in an area where they cannot be overheard.

criteria.criterion_text: The care for each patient is planned, provided and noted in the patient’s record.
criteria.guideline: The need for ongoing education and educational support must be included when planning the care of children in the service. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The patient's response to care and therapeutic interventions is documented in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Meetings to discuss patient care/treatment regimes are held by the multidisciplinary team at regular intervals as determined by organisational policy and documented in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: All procedures and diagnostic tests ordered and performed, are documented in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: This documented evidence may include nurses' and doctors' notes as well as printed reports, which are filed in the patient record. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The results of procedures and diagnostic tests performed are available in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: This criterion will be scored PC if the results are not initialled by the doctor, the treatment plan does not indicate action taken, or the nursing notes do not indicate that the doctor was informed. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Orders are found in a uniform location in patient records.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Only those permitted to write orders do so.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Reassessments are performed at appropriate, regular intervals and following a change in the patient’s condition, and documented in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: Patients should be reassessed according to clinical need. This may be continuous, hourly or daily (as a minimum), depending on the clinical condition of the patient. Any deterioration in a patient's condition should result in a formal reassessment by an appropriately trained clinician – often a nurse in the first instance followed by a doctor if clinically indicated. The nature of these reassessments and the processes to be followed should be defined in organisation or departmental policy. If the doctors' (or other health care practitioners') clinical notes are not available in the patient's records, this criterion will be scored PC. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Care plans are revised when necessary in response to the findings of reassessments.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: Compassionate care is provided to the dying and to those in pain.
standards.intent:
While pain may be a part of the patient experience, unrelieved pain has adverse physical and psychological effects. The patient's right to appropriate assessment and management of pain is respected and supported. The organisation has processes to:
Identify patients with pain during initial assessment and reassessment
Communicate with, and provide education for, patients and families about pain management in the context of their personal, cultural and religious beliefs
Educate healthcare providers in pain assessment and management.
Dying patients have unique needs for respectful, compassionate care. Concern for the patient's comfort and dignity guides all aspects of care during the final stages of life. To accomplish this, all personnel are made aware of the unique needs of patients at the end of life. These needs include treatment of primary and secondary symptoms; pain management; response to the psychological, social, emotional, religious and cultural concerns of the patient and family; and involvement in care decisions.

criteria.criterion_text: The assessment process makes provision for patients in pain to be identified.
criteria.guideline: This requirement should be included in the assessment policy and procedure for the organisation and must include methods for identifying pain in non-communicating and intellectually disabled patients. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients in pain receive care according to pain management guidelines.
criteria.guideline: There must be evidence that all clinical professionals dealing with the patient's pain management have contributed to the patient’s record. Monitoring of the effectiveness of pain management must be recorded. Evidence could include the documentation of pain using an individualised assessment form and pain scoring charts. Pain management continues with the prescription and administration of medication for pain as well as the follow-up assessment after treatment. These records will also be evaluated. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence that the effectiveness of pain and symptom management is monitored.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and families are educated about pain and pain management.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit and patient interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation has processes to educate health professionals in assessing and managing pain.
criteria.guideline: Training must include must include the identification of pain in non-communicating and intellectually disabled patients. Documented evidence of personnel training will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Personnel provide respectful and compassionate care to dying patients.
criteria.guideline: This includes the organisation respecting the need of the physically ill or dying patient to be transferred to another suitable facility/unit. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit and patient interviews.

standards.standard_title: Policies and procedures guide the care of high-risk patients and the provision of high-risk services
standards.intent:
Some patients are considered "high-risk" because of their age, condition or the critical nature of their needs. Psychiatric patients and especially children, are commonly in this group as they may not speak for themselves, understand the care process or participate in decisions regarding their care.
Policies and procedures are important for personnel to understand high-risk patients and services, and to respond in a thorough, competent and uniform manner. The clinical and managerial leaders take responsibility for identifying the patients and services considered high-risk, using a collaborative process to develop policies and procedures and to train personnel in their implementation.
Of particular concern is that the policies or procedures identify:
How planning will occur
The documentation required for the care team to work effectively
Special consent considerations
Monitoring requirements
Special qualifications or skills of personnel involved in the care process
Clinical guidelines and pathways are frequently helpful and may be incorporated in the process. Monitoring provides the information needed to ensure that the policies and procedures are adequately implemented and followed for all relevant patients and services.
High risk patients and procedures include (where applicable):
Care of emergency patients
Management of patients, especially children in restraint (including the documentation required when a patient is restrained)
Management of patients who may be a danger to themselves or others
Management of the violent patient
Care of frail and dependent children
The management of patients in seclusion (including the documentation required when a patient in treated in seclusion)
The use of behaviour modification techniques
Observation of patients treated for eating disorders

criteria.criterion_text: Documented policies, clinical guidelines or standard operating   procedures for identified high risk patients and procedures, which include items (a)-(h) in the standard intent above as a minimum, are available and readily accessible.
criteria.guideline: The clinical and managerial leaders take responsibility for identifying the patients and services considered high risk, using a collaborative process to develop policies and procedures and training personnel in their implementation. Implementation will be verified during the patient record audit. The 40/80 rule for compliance will apply.

criteria.criterion_text: In-service training is provided to personnel to ensure they understand the intent and content of the policies and procedures.

Where policies and procedures refer to patient care, compliance will be measured against the relevant areas in the patients’ records during the record audit process.By implication, if the policies are implemented, personnel can be considered to have been trained and this criterion will be scored accordingly. This training could take the form of formal in-service training, introduction at orientation to policies, discussions at departmental meetings, case studies, etc.Where policies and procedures refer to patient care, compliance will be measured against the relevant areas in the patients’ records during the record audit process.By implication, if the policies are implemented, personnel can be considered to have been trained and this criterion will be scored accordingly. This training could take the form of formal in-service training, introduction at orientation to policies, discussions at departmental meetings, case studies, etc.
Patients receive care consistent with the policies and procedures.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit and patient interviews.

standards.standard_title: Adequate information is provided when obtaining informed consent from patients or their legal representatives
standards.intent:
Patients and their families or decision-makers must receive adequate information to participate in care decisions by being informed as to what tests, procedures and treatments require consent and how they can give consent, for example verbally, by signing a consent form, or through some other mechanism. Patients and families understand who may, in addition to the patient, give consent.
Organisation policy must define the situations under which informed consent is required and specify the type of consent required, for example, written or verbal consent.
The leaders must agree and implement a standardised procedure for obtaining informed consent which meets all requirements of country-specific legislation and includes:
Discussion of the patient's diagnosis and why the procedure is advised
The identification and designation of the person who is authorised to perform the procedure
A thorough explanation of the proposed procedure in a clear and understandable manner
The expected benefits of the procedure
The likelihood of success of the procedure
The expected course of recovery following the procedure
The potential risks and complications of the procedure
A discussion of viable alternative options including risks and benefits
The potential risks of refusing the proposed procedure
Confirmation that the patient or their legal representative has understood the information provided
An opportunity for the patient or their legal representative to ask questions
Comprehensive documentation of the process followed to obtain informed consent.
How to obtain consent if the patient is unable to give consent due to age, diminished mental capacity (for example, delirium, learning difficulties, etc.) or by virtue of their physical illness (for example, comatose)
Documentation of the signature of the patient or their legal representative for written consent
Documentation of verbal consent in the patient record
The procedure for obtaining informed consent should acknowledge and accommodate religious, cultural and social needs of patients and their families.
The leaders must agree and implement a process for the documentation of verbal consent. Where written consent is required, it can be useful to include the steps in the process in the consent form with space allocated for signature of the patient and the personnel member providing the information to confirm that the step has been completed satisfactorily.
Personnel responsible for obtaining informed consent must receive training on the agreed policies and procedures relating to informed consent to ensure that they are fully aware of organization requirements.
Consent should only be obtained by suitably trained professional personnel who are familiar with the procedure and its risks, complications and alternatives. Ideally, the person who will perform the procedure should be the one to obtain consent for the procedure. Where this is not the case, the patient should be informed of which healthcare professional will be performing the procedure.
Where permissible according to country-specific legislation, organisation personnel who can consent on behalf of patients unable to give independent consent should be identified. This will require a detailed policy and procedure which should reflect the requirements set out in the relevant national legislation. A copy of this legislation should be available to be read in conjunction with the policy.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented process for obtaining of informed consent.
criteria.guideline: The documented process should outline all the steps in a) – o) in the standard intent above as a minimum. An assessment will be made of the provision of an organisational consent form and the completion of this form when indicated. This criterion will be scored NC if written consent is indicated but suitable forms are not used.

criteria.criterion_text: Consent forms are completed comprehensively and available in the patient records.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  This criterion refers to all instances where patients give informed consent. If consent was not required for the patient records audited, this criterion becomes NA. If there is a correctly completed consent form the implication is that the patient has been provided with the information indicated in the relevant criterion above. Where the form is not completed in accordance with policy, this criterion will be scored PC or NC, depending on the average results of the record audit (40/80 rule will apply). The relevant criteria above will receive the same score. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Verbal consent is obtained and recorded according to organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL This criterion refers to all instances where patients give verbal informed consent. If consent was not required for the patient records audited, this criterion becomes NA. If there is a correctly completed verbal consent form the implication is that the patient has been provided with the information indicated in the relevant criterion above. Where the form is not completed in accordance with policy, this criterion will be scored PC or NC, depending on the average results of the record audit (40/80 rule will apply). The relevant criteria above will receive the same score. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and their families or decision-makers receive adequate information in a clear and understandable way to enable them to participate in care decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit and patient interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication Management

categories.category_title: Medication Management

standards.standard_title: Medication is ordered according to organisational policy and stored in a secure and clean environment
standards.intent:
Organisational policy on the ordering and storage of medication must be followed in each patient care area which stores medication for administration to patients under their care. Each organisation must identify those individuals permitted to order medication. These individuals must have the knowledge and experience required by law, registration or regulations to be permitted to order medication. The organisation must identify any additional individuals permitted to order medication in emergency situations.
Medication must be stored in a clean and secure environment that complies with legislation, regulation and professional practice standards.
Medication must be clearly labelled, correctly stored and protected from heat, light and moisture where necessary. Scheduled (controlled) drugs/narcotics/barbiturates and other dangerous drugs must be stored according to country-specific requirements. As a minimum, these drugs must be stored in a locked cupboard or container of substantial construction. "Substantial" construction is interpreted to be a cupboard/container that is mounted to the wall or fixed to the floor. Most often, these are steel cabinets, but solid wooden cupboards are also acceptable. There must be a registry, log or other system to monitor and account for controlled substances, which must be completed at the same time that medication is received, administered or returned.
Refrigeration facilities must be provided for safe storage of certain medication. There must be a process to ensure that thermolabile medication has been stored and transported at the correct temperature throughout the life of the medication. Refrigerators must be cleaned regularly and defrosted when necessary. Doors, hinges and seals must all be functional.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is ordered according to organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: The organisational policy for ordering medication should be readily available to unit personnel. Documentation relating to the ordering of medication will be assessed for compliance to the organisational policy.

criteria.criterion_text: All storage areas for medication and pharmaceutical supplies comply with current pharmaceutical acts and regulations and manufacturer guidelines (for example, security, temperature, light and humidity control).
criteria.guideline: Note must be made of provision for medication that should not be exposed to direct sunlight. Where the ambient temperature in the cupboard/room cannot be maintained at 20-25 degrees Celsius there should be evidence that the environmental temperature is monitored and complies with the manufacturer’s requirements for medication storage.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is stored in a locked storage device or cabinet that is accessible only to authorised personnel.
criteria.guideline: All medication storage areas (trolleys, cupboards, rooms, refrigerators etc.) will be examined for compliance. An unlocked refrigerator will only be accepted if it is in a locked room with access limited to relevant personnel only. An unlocked refrigerator in an unlocked room will be scored as NC.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication identified for special control by legislation or organisational policy is stored in a cabinet of substantial construction, for which only authorised personnel have the keys.
criteria.guideline: Country-specific laws and regulations or organisational policy will determine the nature of such medication.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication identified for special control by legislation or organisation policy is accurately accounted for.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Compliance will be measured against the local medication control regulations. Control measures generally include the keeping of registers for these items.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is securely and legibly labelled with relevant information as required by legislation and organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Country-specific regulations apply, but must include at least the following: Name of ward and/or name of patient and patient record number (if applicable) Proprietary name/approved name or name of each active ingredient Direction with regard to the manner in which medication must be used Number of dose units in the container Date of dispensing Expiry date and batch number Additional labels with warnings and storage instructions in accordance with organisation policy

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is stored in a clean environment.
criteria.guideline: This applies to all medication storage areas in the organisation.

criteria.criterion_text: A dedicated refrigerator is available for medication requiring storage at low temperatures.
criteria.guideline: There should be a refrigerator designated for the storage of medication only.

criteria.criterion_text: The temperature of the refrigerator is monitored and recorded according to organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL  Documented evidence in the form of daily temperature recording and monitoring of the refrigerator/s must be available. The temperature must be maintained within a safe range according to manufacturer's specifications. This is usually 2-8°C.

criteria.criterion_text: Appropriate action is taken and recorded when the temperature of the refrigerator is outside the recommended range.
criteria.guideline: The actions taken must be documented and kept on file for reference purposes. Monitoring logs should demonstrate more frequent temperature checks until it is confirmed that the action taken has been successful in restoring maintenance of the required refrigerator temperature range.

criteria.criterion_text: Expiry dates (including those of emergency medication) are checked regularly at defined intervals according to organisational policy and medication is replaced before the expiry date.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be measured against relevant laws and organisation policies.

standards.standard_title: Medication use throughout the organisation complies with applicable laws and regulations.
standards.intent:
Medication management is not only the responsibility of the pharmaceutical service but also of managers and clinical care providers. Medical, nursing, pharmacy and administrative personnel should participate in a collaborative process to develop and monitor policies and procedures. Each organisation must identify those individuals permitted to prescribe medication. These individuals must have the requisite knowledge and experience, and be permitted by law, registration or regulations to prescribe medication. The organisation must identify any additional individuals permitted to prescribe medication in emergency situations. Prescription of medication and verbal medication orders must be issued and documented according to organisational policy.
The safe administration of medication requires a strict and comprehensive policy. The policy relating to the administration of medication into catheters and tubing must include steps to ensure that the tubing is checked prior to administration to ensure that medication is not administered via the wrong route. Only personnel who are suitably trained and experienced must be permitted to administer medication to patients and this responsibility must be documented.
The patient, medical practitioner, nurse and other care providers must work together to monitor patients on medication. The purpose of monitoring is to evaluate the patient's response to medication, adjust the dosage or type of medication when needed and identify, record and report any adverse effects the patient may suffer as a result of the medication.
Doctors, nurses and pharmacists are expected to report adverse reactions that are suspected to be related to medication, irrespective of whether the reaction is well recognised, potentially serious or clinically insignificant. The organisation must ensure that country-specific requirements for the reporting of adverse drug reactions are followed.
In addition to the reporting of adverse drug reactions, there must be a system for the reporting and investigation of medication errors. The system must focus on the prevention of medication errors through understanding the types of errors that occur. Each error should be investigated to understand why it happened. Where necessary, processes should be adapted to incorporate measures which will reduce the likelihood of the error happening again. Personnel training, either in existing or adapted medication administration processes, can be beneficial in preventing future errors. Pharmacy personnel should participate in such training interventions.
Only personnel who are suitably trained and experienced may administer medication to patients. The responsibility of these persons for medication administration is documented.

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that guide the safe prescribing and administration of medication are implemented.
criteria.guideline: These policies should be developed by all role-players i.e. medical practitioners, nursing personnel and pharmacists. Country-specific regulations will apply. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Only those permitted by the organisation and by relevant laws and regulations prescribe medication.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Only those permitted by the organisation and by relevant laws and regulations administer medication.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a process to place limits, when appropriate, on the prescribing or ordering practices of individuals.
criteria.guideline: This is particularly relevant where certain medication may only be prescribed by designated specialists. Compliance will be verified with reference to organisational policy and country-specific legislation.

criteria.criterion_text: Relevant personnel are trained in correct prescribing, ordering and administration practices.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during personnel interviews and training records in personnel files.

criteria.criterion_text: On admission, all current medication taken by the patient is documented in the patient record, including herbal and over-the-counter medication.
criteria.guideline: Implementation of the organisational policy is required. This includes traditional and homeopathic medication. These should be re-prescribed on the prescription sheet if indicated. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Verbal and telephonic medication prescriptions are documented according to organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: The procedure to be followed must be clearly documented in line with current legislation and implemented. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit

criteria.criterion_text: There is evidence that patients are identified before medication is administered.
criteria.guideline: This will be assessed in relation to the existing policy framework, including the use of patient identification bands when required. Compliance will also be confirmed while observing medication administration practices and during personnel interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication is checked against the original prescriptions and administered as prescribed.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL Compliance could be assessed by observing the administration of medication while visiting the clinical departments. During the patient record audit process, check for evidence of transcribing by nurses from the original prescription onto the medication administration sheet.

criteria.criterion_text: The medication prescribed for and administered to each patient is recorded.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: 11Healthcare professionals monitor medication effects on patients collaboratively.

criteria.criterion_text: Therapeutic plasma medication concentrations are checked in accordance with manufacturers' guidelines and organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: While clinical signs and symptoms are useful measures of medication therapy efficacy, additional information may be required by utilising plasma medication concentration levels during the initiation and maintenance of certain medication. The organisational policy, manufacturer's guidelines and medical practitioner's orders identify those instances in which therapeutic medication levels must be obtained. Compliance is verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Adverse drug reactions (ADR) are observed, recorded and reported through a process and within a time frame defined by the organisation.
criteria.guideline: An adverse drug reaction (ADR) is an expression that describes harm associated with the use of given medication at a normal dose. The meaning of this expression differs from the meaning of 'side effect', as this last expression might also imply that the effects can be beneficial. The study of ADRs is the concern of the field known as pharmacovigilance. Compliance is verified during the patient record audit. Evidence will be sought of documentation reporting the reaction to the physician and the pharmacist.

criteria.criterion_text: Medication errors are reported through a process and within a time frame defined by the organisation.
criteria.guideline: A process must be in place for the recording of medication errors. These include errors in relation to prescribing, dispensing and administration of medication.

criteria.criterion_text: Food and Nutrition Therapy

categories.category_title: Food and Nutrition Therapy

standards.standard_title: Food and nutrition therapy appropriate for the patient and consistent with his or her clinical care is regularly available.
standards.intent:
A qualified caregiver orders appropriate food or other nutrients. The patient participates in planning and selecting foods, and the patient's family may, when appropriate, participate in providing food. They are educated as to which foods are contraindicated, including information about any medication associated with food interactions. When possible, patients are offered a variety of food choices consistent with their nutritional status.
The nutritional status of the patients is monitored.

criteria.criterion_text: Food appropriate to the patient is regularly available.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified from interviews with personnel and patients especially regarding times of meals and the availability of special diets. Menus and meal times will be checked.

criteria.criterion_text: Food orders appropriate for the patient’s nutritional status and needs are documented in the patient’s record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit, especially as it relates to the patient’s assessment, the care plan and the identification of special diet requirements. Prescribed diets must be ordered and delivered to the patient.

criteria.criterion_text: Wherever possible, patient food preferences are respected and substitutions made available
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient food preferences consider cultural and religious requirements.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and with reference to cultural and religious requirements identified during the initial assessment of the patient.

criteria.criterion_text: When families provide food, they are educated about the patient's dietary limitations.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients assessed as being at nutritional risk receive nutrition therapy.
criteria.guideline: Such patients could include those unable to take normal meals for example, unable to swallow, specific disease processes, etc. This could include the provision of nutritional supplements or specialised diets. This criterion and those that follow apply only to nutrition therapy. Compliance of all these criteria will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: A collaborative process is used to plan, deliver and monitor nutrition therapy.
criteria.guideline: This should include the involvement of a dietician if available.  Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Nutrition therapy provided, whether oral, enteral or parenteral, is documented in the patient’s record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Response to nutrition therapy is monitored and recorded.
criteria.guideline: This may take the form of weight/mass checks, laboratory tests for protein and/or lipid levels etc.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient and Family Education

categories.category_title: Patient and Family Education

standards.standard_title: Each patient's educational needs are assessed and written in their record.
standards.intent:
Education is focused on the specific knowledge and skills the patient and his or her family will need to make care decisions, participate in care, and continue care at home. Variables like educational literacy, beliefs and limitations are taken into account.
The organisation decides the placement and format for educational assessment, planning and delivery of information in the patient's record.
Education is provided to support care decisions of patients and families. In addition, when a patient or family directly participate in providing care, for example changing dressings, feeding and administration of medication, they need to be educated.
On occasion, it is important that they be aware of any financial implications associated with care choices, such as choosing to remain an inpatient rather than an outpatient.
Education in areas that carry high risk to patients is routinely provided by the organisation, for instance safe and effective use of medications and medical equipment.
Community organisations that support health promotion and disease prevention education are identified, and, when possible, ongoing relationships are established.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and families' educational needs are assessed and recorded.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a uniform process for the recording of patient education information.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and families are educated about participation in the care process.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and families are informed about the financial implications of care decisions.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and their families are educated about their diagnosis and relevant health risks.
criteria.guideline: For example, safe use of medication and medical equipment, medication and food interaction, therapeutic diet and food interactions, defaulting on medication use, etc. Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation identifies and establishes relationships with community resources that support continuing mental and physical health promotion and disease prevention education.
criteria.guideline: Evidence of meetings will be required.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and families are referred to these organisations as appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits.

standards.standard_title: Education methods consider the patient’s and family’s values and preferences.
standards.intent:
Learning occurs when attention is paid to the methods used to educate patients and families. The organisation selects appropriate educational methods and people to provide the education.
Personnel collaboration helps to ensure that the information patients and families receive is comprehensive, consistent, and as effective as possible.
40.6.2Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: The patient and family are taught in a language and format that they can understand.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Those who provide education have the knowledge and communication skills for effective education.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient interviews and personnel training records.

criteria.criterion_text: Health professionals caring for the patient work collaboratively when appropriate.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Interaction between personnel, the patient and family is noted in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Continuity of Care

categories.category_title: Continuity of Care

standards.standard_title: The organisation designs and implements processes to provide continuity of patient care services within the organisation and coordination among health professionals.
standards.intent:
As patients move through a healthcare organisation from admission to discharge or transfer, several departments, services and healthcare providers may be involved in providing care. Without coordination and effective transfer of information and responsibilities, errors of omission and commission are more likely to occur, exposing the patient to avoidable risks. The organisation should therefore document and implement procedures to minimise the likelihood of these errors occurring.
40.7.1Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that guide the movement of patients within the organisation are implemented.
criteria.guideline: This refers to transfers between departments within the organisation as well as admissions from the out-patient department. Please take note that a transfer to theatre/procedure room is also regarded as an internal transfer. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Individuals responsible for the patient's care and its coordination are identified for all phases of patient care.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Continuity and coordination are evident throughout all phases of patient care.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: When a patient is transferred within the organisation, they are accompanied by their patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient handover between healthcare professionals is standardised according to organisational policy.
criteria.guideline: The complexity and comprehensiveness of the content of handover information required to achieve continuity of care will differ for different types of handover situations. The details to be included in each situation should be agreed upon by all stakeholders and formalised in the organisational policy framework to ensure a standardised approach at all times. The transfer of information can be verbal for low risk situations, but should be documented, preferably by the use of forms, for high risk situations. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: There is a process known to personnel to refer patients for specialised consultation, investigations and/or treatment at other healthcare organisations.
standards.intent:
Medical practitioners may need to refer patients to other facilities for a secondary consultation to confirm an opinion, access more extensive diagnostic evaluations than may be available locally, or access specialised treatment that the referring organisation may be unable to provide.
This standard refers to the process by which a patient is referred to another healthcare organisation only to access services not offered by the referring organisation and then returns to the referring organisation to receive ongoing care, either as part of their care as an inpatient (in which case they will return to the organisation following their review at the referral organisation) or while under the care of the outpatient department. Organisational policy must clearly describe this referral process.
40.7.2Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures that guide the movement of patients for referral to another organisation are implemented.
criteria.guideline: The organisation must clearly describe the referral process, especially where patients are sent to another healthcare organisation for specialist consultation or special investigations and then return to the referring organisation. Note that this criterion does not refer to the transfer of a patient to another healthcare organisation to take over the care of the patient. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients are accompanied and monitored by an appropriately qualified person during the process.
criteria.guideline: This should include the cadre of personnel suitable for the level of care required by the patient to be transported.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the referral note is available in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Follow-up care based on the findings of investigations and/or consultations performed outside the referring organisation is  documented in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: There is a process to transfer patients to another organisation to meet their continuing needs.
standards.intent:
This standard refers to the process by which a patient is discharged from the transferring organisation and handed over to receive ongoing care at another healthcare organisation.
Transfer may be to a higher level of care for specialised consultation and/or treatment, or for less intensive services such as sub-acute care or long-term rehabilitation. To ensure continuity of care, adequate information must accompany the patient.
Transfer may be an uncomplicated process with the patient alert and talking, or may involve continuous nursing or medical supervision. The qualifications of the individual accompanying the patient must be appropriate to the level of care required during transfer. The process for transferring the patient must consider transportation needs as well as clinical needs.
40.7.3Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented process for transferring patients to other organisations.
criteria.guideline: The documented process should be available in the unit and readily accessible to unit personnel. The process must include: Guidance on how to make the necessary transport arrangements The qualifications required in the clinical personnel accompanying the patient for various levels of clinical care

criteria.criterion_text: When a patient is transferred to another organisation, the receiving organisation is given a written summary of the patient's clinical condition and the interventions performed by the referring organisation.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: A copy of the transfer summary is available in the patient record.
criteria.guideline: CRITICAL Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The transferring organisation documents the transfer in the organisation's patient record.
criteria.guideline: Documentation must include as a minimum: The reason(s) for the transfer Any special conditions related to transfer The condition of the patient before transfer The name of the healthcare organisation receiving the patient Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The referring organisation determines that the receiving organisation can meet the patient’s continuing care needs, and establishes arrangements to ensure continuity.
criteria.guideline: This should be done by the departmental management team when referral pathways for the unit are established. These pathways should be documented along with all relevant details, for example, transport arrangements and contact details. The documented pathway should be readily available to personnel who will be responsible for making the decision to transfer patients to another organisation.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients are accompanied and monitored by an appropriately qualified person during transfer.
criteria.guideline: This should include the cadre of personnel suitable for the level of care required by the patient to be transferred.

standards.standard_title: There is an organised process to discharge patients.
standards.intent:
Care planning should include arrangements to meet the patients' continuing needs after discharge as early in the care process as possible. Instructions for discharge and follow-up visits must be clear and provided in writing.
The discharge summary is one of the most important documents for ensuring continuity of care and facilitating correct management at subsequent visits. Information provided by the organisation may include when to resume daily activities, preventive practices relevant to the patient's condition and, when appropriate, information on coping with disease or disability. At discharge, patients should be alerted to symptoms and signs related to their diagnosis which require urgent medical attention and how to access healthcare services should they develop such symptoms or signs. This process is referred to as "safety-netting".
The discharge summary must contain the following content as a minimum:
The diagnosis of main and significant illnesses and reason for admission
The results of investigations that will influence further management
All procedures performed
All significant findings
The patient's condition at discharge
Discharge medication
Follow-up arrangements where appropriate, including emergency review
40.7.4Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: There is a documented process to discharge patients.
criteria.guideline: This process must include any criteria or conditions that the child and family need to meet prior to discharge. The documented process must be available in the unit and readily accessible to, and known by organisational personnel.

criteria.criterion_text: Where the initial assessment indicates that discharge planning will be required, planning requirements are included in the patient's care plan and completed as scheduled.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation works with the family, caregivers, healthcare practitioners and agencies outside the organisation to ensure timely and appropriate discharge and continuity of care.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during patient record audits.

criteria.criterion_text: Patients and where appropriate their families or caregivers are given understandable follow-up instructions which are documented in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: The follow-up instructions may be provided to the patient by the medical practitioner, or by the nurse following instructions from the doctor. The instructions must include as a minimum: Medication information Possible complications Return for follow-up care When and where to obtain urgent care Compliance will be verified through patient record audits and patient interviews.

criteria.criterion_text: A discharge summary, which includes (a)-(g) in the standard intent above as a minimum, is written by the medical practitioner  when each patient is discharged.
criteria.guideline: This criterion and the next apply to the discharge summary completed by the medical practitioner and not to the nurses' discharge note. Where a discharge summary has only been partially completed, i.e. does not include all items (a)-(f) it will be scored PC. A nurse's discharge note is not acceptable and is scored NC. The 40/80 rule for compliance will apply. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Each record contains a copy of the discharge summary.
criteria.guideline: This will ensure that the discharge summary is available for follow-up visits. Compliance will be verified during patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: When appropriate the patient is given a copy of the discharge summary.
criteria.guideline: This could be appropriate if the patient's care will be continued outside of the country.

criteria.criterion_text: Quality Improvement

categories.category_title: Quality Improvement

standards.standard_title: A formalised proactive quality improvement approach is maintained in the  child and family service.
standards.intent:
The senior management team is responsible for ensuring that standards are set throughout the organisation. Within each department or service, unit managers should ensure that standards are set for the particular unit. Departmental or service managers should use available data and information to identify priority areas for quality monitoring and improvement. This should be done in collaboration with the organisation's central quality management structures to ensure coordinated quality improvement activities throughout the organisation.
Quality monitoring is typically applied to high risk, high volume or high cost activities, or areas of concern identified by personnel, patients or visitors. Some examples of activities that may benefit from quality monitoring include:
Patient assessment
Procedures carried out
The use of medication
Medication errors
Patient and family expectations and satisfaction
The following will be evaluated:
The manner in which problems were identified and prioritised in this service for which quality improvement activities were initiated
The processes put in place to resolve the problems
The identification of indicators to measure improvement
The tool(s) used to evaluate these indicators
The monitoring of these indicators and corrective steps taken when goals were not achieved
Graphed and/or tabled results, as appropriate
A once-off project such as acquiring a specific item of equipment will be scored NC. Quality improvement processes not related to the clinical quality of patient care but to the environment within which care is provided, for example, monitoring the checking of the emergency trolley over time, will be scored PC.

criteria.criterion_text: There is a written quality improvement programme for the child and family service that is developed and agreed upon by the personnel of the service.
criteria.guideline: The minimum requirement for compliance will be the availability of evidence of: At least one clinical audit Participation in documentation (patient record) audits Participation in the monitoring of near misses, adverse events and sentinel events

criteria.criterion_text: Indicators of performance are identified to evaluate the quality of treatment and patient care.

As the emphasis is on the quality of patient care, this cannot be compliant if there is no evidence of clinical audits.These could take the form of evaluation of the implementation of clinical guidelines, case discussions, reports on negative incidents, etc. An indicator, in this context, is a measure used to determine improvements in clinical care over time. The information gained must be used to identify and implement improvement processes. Statistical reports without documented evidence of continuous quality improvement will be scored PC.As the emphasis is on the quality of patient care, this cannot be compliant if there is no evidence of clinical audits.These could take the form of evaluation of the implementation of clinical guidelines, case discussions, reports on negative incidents, etc. An indicator, in this context, is a measure used to determine improvements in clinical care over time. The information gained must be used to identify and implement improvement processes. Statistical reports without documented evidence of continuous quality improvement will be scored PC.
Processes are selected in order of priority for evaluation and improvement in the quality of treatment and care.
criteria.guideline: Quality monitoring is typically applied to high risk, high volume or high cost activities, or areas of concern identified by personnel, patients or visitors.

criteria.criterion_text: The quality improvement cycle includes the monitoring and evaluation of the standards set, and remedial action implemented.
criteria.guideline: The quality improvement cycle does not stop with the implementation of a quality improvement plan. Ongoing monitoring of the process or procedure under consideration is required, with evaluation of the monitoring data collected to ensure that the plan has achieved its intended aim. If not, the quality improvement cycle should be repeated, incorporating the learning from previous attempts, until the desired aim is achieved.

criteria.criterion_text: A documentation audit system is in place.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence of such audits must be provided at departmental level. Where the documentation (patient record) audit is an organisation-wide multidisciplinary process, which includes all clinical departments, this criterion will be scored according to the findings of the involvement of each department in the process. Evaluation of results and remedial action taken must be documented as well as maintenance of achieved improvements over time.

criteria.criterion_text: An incident managing system for monitoring near misses/adverse events/sentinel events is implemented, which includes the documentation of responses to recorded incidents and interventions to prevent recurrence of the incident or minimise harm in the event of a recurrence.
criteria.guideline: Participation at department level in the organisation's overall system of the monitoring of near misses/adverse events/sentinel events will be evaluated. Analysed data, with responses/remedial action related to departmental incidents are required.

criteria.criterion_text: Patient Rights

categories.category_title: 1The organisation is responsible for providing processes that support  patient and family rights during care.
1There is a comprehensive infection prevention and control programme   encompassing patient care and employee health.
1The organisation directs and controls risk management programmes, which include health and safety programmes that comply with legislation.
General

standards.standard_title: Clinical practice guidelines are used to guide patient care and reduce unwanted variation.
standards.intent:
Clinical practice guidelines provide a means for improving quality and assist practitioners in making clinical decisions. Consideration should be given to providing guidelines for high risk, high volume and high cost conditions as these are the areas that represent the highest risk to patients and the organisation. In addition, guidelines should be available for conditions which are rarely seen but may have severe consequences for patients if misdiagnosed or mismanaged.
Guidelines are found in the literature under many names including practice parameters, practice guidelines, patient care protocols, standards of practice, care pathways, etc.
Regardless of the source, the scientific basis of guidelines should be reviewed and approved by the clinical leaders and clinical practitioners before implementation. This will ensure that they meet the criteria established by the leaders and are adapted to the community, patient needs and organisation resources. Once adopted, guidelines should be reviewed on a regular basis to ensure their continued relevance.

criteria.criterion_text: Evidence-based clinical practice guidelines relevant to the patients and services of the organisation are available to guide patient care processes.
criteria.guideline: Sometimes such evidence is available for nursing practices only, in which case this will be scored PC. In the absence of guidelines this criterion will be scored NC.

criteria.criterion_text: Organisational and clinical leaders set criteria to select clinical practice guidelines.
criteria.guideline: Every effort should be made to identify and adopt guidelines that have been rigorously and scientifically developed, internationally accepted and adapted for local use.

criteria.criterion_text: The implementation of guidelines is monitored as part of a structured clinical audit.
criteria.guideline: Clinical audits cannot be done on ALL guidelines, but evidence must be available that the management of high cost, high risk, high volume and problem-prone conditions are considered for auditing. If guidelines are available but structured clinical audits are not done, this criterion is scored NC. Sometimes such evidence exists for nursing practices only, in which case this will be scored PC.

criteria.criterion_text: Guidelines are reviewed on a regular basis and updated when necessary.
criteria.guideline: Organisational policy should define the frequency with which guidelines are to be reviewed to ensure they reflect current practice. This should include updating of guidelines following major changes in best practice advice as well as routine review.

criteria.criterion_text: Assessment of Patients

categories.category_title: Assessment of Patients

standards.standard_title: All patients cared for by the organisation have their healthcare needs identified through an established assessment process within appropriate time frames
standards.intent:
When a patient enters a department, the specific information required and the procedures for obtaining and documenting it depend on the patient's needs and on the setting in which care is being provided.
Certain patients may require a modified assessment, for example, very young patients, the frail or elderly, those terminally ill or in pain, patients suspected of drug and/or alcohol dependence and victims of abuse and neglect. When appropriate, the assessment process should be modified to respect local cultural practices.
The organisation defines, in writing, the scope and content of assessments to be performed by each clinical discipline within its scope of practice and applicable laws and regulations.
These findings are used throughout the care process to evaluate patient progress and understand the need for reassessment. It is essential that assessments are documented well and can be easily retrieved from the patient's record.
The healthcare organisation determines the time frame for completing assessments. This may vary in the different settings within the organisation.
When the medical assessment was conducted by a different healthcare organisation, a legible copy of the findings should be filed in the patient's record. Any significant changes in the patient's condition since this assessment should be recorded.
41.2.1Criteria

criteria.criterion_text: Written policies and procedures for assessing patients and their families on admission and during ongoing care are implemented.
criteria.guideline: ROOT  A policy must be available which defines the scope of practice for each discipline, together with specific information required, procedures to be carried out and all documentation to be completed during the assessment of patients and their families. The provision of specifically designed assessment forms for each relevant discipline is considered as evidence of the implementation of such a policy. Where the required information, according to the patient's presenting complaints/condition is not documented, the score will be adjusted accordingly. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Only those individuals permitted by applicable laws, regulations and professional registration perform the assessments.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit, which will focus on the legibility of signatures and the identification of designations/qualifications. The absence of notes made by the doctor in the patient record, or other relevant clinical personnel other than nursing personnel will affect the outcome of the audit process.

criteria.criterion_text: The scope and content of assessment by each discipline is defined.
criteria.guideline: ROOT  It is accepted that the scope and content of assessment for each discipline is defined if the organisation makes use of standardised formats of clinical assessment forms for specific use by different disciplines. The absence of medical notes or other relevant clinical personnel other than nursing personnel in the patient record will affect the outcome of the audit process. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Assessments are performed within appropriate time frames and are comprehensively documented in the patient's records according to organisation policy.
criteria.guideline: Time frames for the assessment of patients should be established for each discipline for example, there should be an indication of which cases are urgent/or not priority cases. It is important to note that this criterion does not refer to the length of time that it takes to perform the assessment, but the time within which the initial assessment should be commenced and/or completed. These time frames should be stated in the policy framework. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The date and time of assessment can be identified.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The organisation identifies those patient populations and special situations for which the assessment process is modified.
criteria.guideline: Documented evidence in the form of a detailed policy and procedure or guideline is provided. To ensure coordination of patient care, all members of the team should be included in the formulation of this policy framework, especially doctors and nurses. Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Those special patient populations receive individualised assessments.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: The findings of assessments performed outside the organisation are verified on admission.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

criteria.criterion_text: Any significant changes in the patient's condition since the assessment performed outside of the organisation are noted in the patient's record.
criteria.guideline: Compliance will be verified during the patient record audit.

standards.standard_title: ECT specific policies and procedures are implemented.
standards.intent:
Guidelines of professional societies and associations are available and followed wherever anaesthesia is administered. This includes nursing personnel who assist the anaesthetist and who monitor the recovery of patients. Implementing these guidelines is particularly important with regard to the qualifications, training and experience required by personnel members in the service, and also the provision, maintenance and use of medical equipment and drugs.
Controlling bodies also develop guidelines and regulations relating to professional practice. Policies and procedures are necessary to guide the administration of the ECT service to ensure the smooth operation of the service, and to ensure that personnel act swiftly and in a coordinated manner in an emergency. These are made available to all unit, recovery room and anaesthetic personnel, and are known and implemented. Biohazards which need to be monitored and notified include electrical hazards.

criteria.criterion_text: Written policies and procedures guide the activities of the ECT treatment room.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, policies and procedures relating to the following are required for compliance with this criterion: The duties of ECT treatment room nursing personnel ECT treatment room cleaning Notification of biohazards Medication control Patient positioning

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures are implemented in relation to the preparation of patients for electro- convulsive therapy.

As a minimum, policies and procedures relating to the following are required for compliance with this criterion:Scheduling of patients for proceduresInformed consentPatient identificationVerification of the treatmentVerification of the last oral intakeEquipment requiredDocumented of processes during care to ensure coordination and safetyAs a minimum, policies and procedures relating to the following are required for compliance with this criterion:Scheduling of patients for proceduresInformed consentPatient identificationVerification of the treatmentVerification of the last oral intakeEquipment requiredDocumented of processes during care to ensure coordination and safety
Policies and procedures are implemented in relation to the anaesthetic service.
criteria.guideline: As a minimum, policies and procedures relating to the following are required for compliance with this criterion: Qualifications of persons who administer anaesthetics Anaesthetic equipment hazards Assessing the fitness of patients to leave the recovery area

criteria.criterion_text: Policies and procedures comply with current guidelines of the local/international professional society of anaesthesiologists.
criteria.guideline: The guidelines used in the service must be available.

criteria.criterion_text: Anaesthetic Care

categories.category_title: Anaesthetic Care

standards.standard_title: A pre-anaesthetic assessment is conducted and recorded
standards.intent:
Psychiatric patients sometimes receive anaesthesia for electroconvulsive therapy.
The preoperative anaesthesia assessment determines if the patient is a suitable candidate for the planned anaesthetic. The clinical assessment and results of investigations must be available to the doctor performing the assessment.
Because anaesthesia carries a high level of risk, its administration must be carefully planned. The patient's pre-anaesthetic assessment is the basis for that plan. The pre- anaesthetic assessment provides information required to:
Select the anaesthesia and plan anaesthesia care
Identify any drug sensitivities
Safely administer an appropriate anaesthetic
Interpret findings of patient monitoring
An anaesthesiologist or other qualified individual conducts the pre-anaesthetic assessment. Anaesthetic care is carefully planned and documented in the anaesthetic record. The plan considers information from the assessment of other health professionals in the multi- disciplinary team and identifies the anaesthetic to be used, the method of administration, other medication and fluids, monitoring procedures, and anticipated post-anaesthetic care.
The anaesthetic planning process includes education of the patient and his or her family or decision-maker on the risks, potential complications and options related to the planned anaesthetic and postoperative analgesia. This discussion occurs as part of the process to obtain consent for anaesthesia. The anaesthesiologist or the qualified individual who will administer anaesthesia provides this education.
41.3.1Criteria
